ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for buttered corn cup; mapped to sweet corn food product as closest defensible ancestor. Butter component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (sweet corn, cooked) used as closest defensible match. Butter addition not represented in FoodEx2 base code; noted as preparation modifier.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for buttered corn cup preparation; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one cup (150 g) of steamed or boiled sweet corn kernels topped with a pat of butter, as commonly sold at Singapore hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Corn cup (with butter) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14060004 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this source. Potassium value of 2.0 mg/100g is anomalously low compared to typical sweet corn (~270 mg/100g) and is flagged in notes; the HPB-reported value is preserved as-is per source fidelity policy. Saturated fat of 3.8 g/100g and trans fat of 0.1 g/100g are estimated from butter contribution based on USDA FoodData Central butter composition data, as HPB does not report these sub-fractions separately.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 is based on sweet corn (boiled/steamed) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Butter addition slows gastric emptying and may modestly lower effective GI; GI of 52 is used as a conservative estimate for the buttered preparation. GL per serving calculated as: GI x available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 52 x (16.75 x 1.50) / 100 = 52 x 25.125 / 100 = 13.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (138 mg/100g) and butter-derived saturated fat; anomalously low potassium (2 mg/100g) per HPB data
hypertension.guidance: Corn cup with butter is acceptable for hypertensive individuals at one-cup portions (150 g, approximately 207 mg sodium); request no added salt during preparation and consider requesting less butter to reduce saturated fat intake, noting that the reported potassium value of 2 mg/100g is unusually low and may underestimate the actual potassium contribution of sweet corn.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (16.75 g/100g) with meaningful dietary fibre (2.78 g/100g); GI approximately 52 (low-medium); butter slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume one cup (150 g, approximately 25 g available carbohydrate, GL approximately 13) as part of a balanced meal; the dietary fibre and fat from butter moderate glucose absorption, but portion control is essential and sweet sauces or condiments should be avoided to prevent glycaemic excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (approximately 3.8 g/100g estimated) and trace trans fat (approximately 0.1 g/100g estimated); cholesterol 16.52 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of corn cup with butter due to its saturated fat and estimated trace trans fat content from butter; requesting a reduced-butter or olive-oil preparation would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile, and one-cup portions should not be consumed daily.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory saturated fat and estimated trace trans fat from butter
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Corn cup with butter is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to butter's saturated and trace trans fat content which promote systemic inflammation; plain steamed or boiled corn without butter, or substitution with extra-virgin olive oil, is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Corn Cup with Butter is a popular Singaporean hawker and food court snack consisting of steamed or boiled sweet corn kernels served in a disposable cup and topped with a pat of butter. The butter melts over the warm corn, creating a rich, slightly sweet, and savoury flavour profile with a tender, juicy kernel texture. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast item or light snack throughout the day at hawker centres, food courts, and pasar malam night markets across Singapore. Per 100 g, the dish provides 145 kcal, 3.76 g protein, 6.78 g fat (including approximately 3.8 g saturated fat from butter and an estimated 0.1 g trans fat), 16.75 g carbohydrate, 2.78 g dietary fibre, and 138 mg sodium. Sugar content is very low at 0.05 g per 100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low-medium range), and a standard one-cup serving of 150 g yields a glycemic load of approximately 13. The dietary fibre from corn and the fat from butter together moderate postprandial glucose absorption, making this a reasonable occasional snack for individuals managing type 2 diabetes when portion-controlled. However, the saturated fat and trace trans fat from butter raise concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients. The potassium value reported by HPB at 2 mg per 100 g is anomalously low relative to typical sweet corn values of approximately 270 mg per 100 g and may reflect a data limitation. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal, but contains dairy from butter and is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy allergies.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with cr_id F14060004, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled sweet corn. Saturated fat and trans fat sub-fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central butter composition as HPB does not report these separately.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source for this preparation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid sub-fraction and moisture data for this specific corn cup preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this record. Potassium value of 2.0 mg/100g as reported by HPB is anomalously low compared to typical sweet corn (~270 mg/100g per USDA FoodData Central); the HPB value is preserved as-is per source fidelity policy but flagged as a probable data limitation. Saturated fat of 3.8 g/100g and trans fat of 0.1 g/100g are estimated from the butter component using USDA FoodData Central butter composition data (NDB 01001) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions. The food is classified as Singaporean cuisine as it is a ubiquitous hawker snack in Singapore, though sweet corn with butter is not uniquely Singaporean in origin.
